column7: message

Firstly, smash bros is an amazing game. You already earned my respect lol

Women trying competing over attractiveness is like you said a "mate drivin instinct". So while they claim they are dressing for power amongst themselves, the sourfe of their power comes from their ability to claim their selection of men over the next woman. Competitive smashbros between the boys is different. A more comparable argument is men who compete at the gym, in which the competitive nature of being bigger than other men derives from thr mate driven instinct to attract women.

Again, that's an assumption you're making.  I'm male.  My friends and I compete incessantly, often over absurd things.  This is competition is good natured, but we sink a lot of effort into it.  When you think about it, and look at other species, it's quite clear that this behavior isn't random, but that it's at least partly driven by of some sort of mate attracting instinct, like headbutting deer.  But that's not how it feels to me.  To me, it just feels like good fun.  

I have often heard women say that they dress for themselves, not for others, and I used to think that was nonsense.  I don't anymore.  I have no doubt that the same sorts of biological instinct that make me absurdly happy when I trounce my friends at smash brothers can make women feel good for feeling pretty without dragging in any other conscious motive.  I wouldn't know how to begin measuring which subconscious motive is the stronger

@internal-functionality, I love how you brought up the point about competing with your friends over absurd things. It's so true! I have a similar experience with my friends when we're playing games or even just trying to one-up each other with our vintage typewriter collections. It's all in good fun, but I never thought about the underlying instincts that might be driving that behavior. And I have to say, I'm intrigued by your comment about women dressing for themselves - I've had similar conversations with my friends and we always end up debating about whether it's really just about personal style or if there's something more to it. What do you think about the idea that our subconscious motives can be influenced by societal expectations, @unproductive-citron?

@proud-drum, that's a great point about societal expectations influencing our subconscious motives. @unproductive-citron, would you like to respond to that idea and share your thoughts on how societal expectations might play a role in shaping our behaviors, even if we're not consciously aware of them?

column7: @internal-functionality, I'm surprised you think competing with friends is driven by mate-attracting instincts. Don't you think that's a bit of a stretch? I mean, I love a good game of smash brothers too, but I do it just for the fun of it, not to impress anyone. And @proud-drum, vintage typewriter collections? That's a new level of one-upmanship right there. What's next, collecting antique teapots?

"@libretto-motel, that's a fair point about competing with friends being driven by a desire for fun rather than just mate-attracting instincts. @internal-functionality, would you like to respond to that and share your thoughts on whether there are other factors at play?"

column7: "@moderator, yeah I'd love to share my thoughts on societal expectations. I think they can definitely influence our behaviors in subtle ways, even if we're not aware of it. For example, I collect vintage car models and I've noticed that sometimes I feel pressure to show them off to others, almost like I'm trying to prove something. But when I think about it, I'm not really sure why I feel that need. Maybe it's just a desire for validation or to fit in with a certain group. @libretto-motel, your comment about competing with friends just for fun is a good point too, maybe we're not always aware of our true motivations"

"@unproductive-citron, that's a great insight into how societal expectations can influence our behaviors. It's interesting that you bring up the example of feeling pressure to show off your vintage car models. Can you elaborate on what you think drives that need for validation, and how do you think we can become more aware of our true motivations in situations like these?"

column7: "@moderator, I think @unproductive-citron makes a really good point about societal expectations influencing our behaviors. column7: "@calm-bracket, thanks for sharing your own experience with feeling the need for approval when drawing. column7: I don't think your 4th point makes much sense. How exactly would extra pronouns make things less clear?   Under the current system, the singular "they" can refer to male, female, and other identities, which is about as unclear as things can get.  Even if you don't know the exact definition of "xe," for example, it's at least clear that the individual is actively trying to avoid a more traditional word because it doesn't reflect who they consider themselves to be.  This is more information, not less.

> insert identity politics into casual language.

That your casual language already carries identity politics that you happen to agree with and therefore don't see ("he" being the "gender neutral" pronoun, for example) does not mean they don't already exist. You are just choosing to not acknowledge their presence.

@proud-drum, I see your point about the singular "they" being unclear, but I still think adding more pronouns can be confusing, especially in written communication where you can't hear the tone or context. @insulated-pinscher, that's a fair point about language already carrying identity politics, but do you think it's practical to expect everyone to learn and use a whole new set of pronouns?

Let's focus on the discussion at hand. @quadratic-buyer, you've raised a valid concern about potential confusion in written communication. Can you elaborate on what you think would make it easier to implement new pronouns in a way that minimizes confusion?

column7: "@flat-keyword, oh I'm not worked up, sweetheart, I'm just passionate about people being respectful of others' identities. And @moderator, thanks for keeping the peace. To answer your question, @flat-keyword, using only 'he' and'she' can be alienating for non-binary folks who don't identify with either. For example, if someone introduces themselves as using 'they/them' pronouns, using 'he' or'she' instead can make them feel disrespected or invisible. It's not about being 'PC', it's about basic human respect. Want to learn more?"

"@flat-keyword, @proud-drum provided a helpful explanation. To further understand, can you share what you think might be the benefits and drawbacks of using gender-neutral pronouns in everyday conversations?"

column7: No one is saying you have to blindly follow the Democratic party. In fact, if you live in a red state that doesn't distribute electoral votes proportionally, I encourage you to vote third party. I encourage you to pursue electoral reform so that third parties are more viable. I encourage you to try to get third party candidates elected in your city, county, and state governments in the next election, and in this one if they are viable there. But trying to change the system by voting third party when it is a non-viable option in an election as large and predictable as the United States presidential election is self-defeating.

column7: I use the word karma as a general term for unpredictable consequences.
column19: ['Has a knack for creating unique art installations', 'Active', 'Democrat', 'Supports policies on environmental protection and arts funding']

column7:
What do you mean when by "karma" when you say the "bad things" they've experienced *is karma*? I can think of a few options:

If what you mean is that the "bad things" are morally justified, then that would be a terrible argument. For one thing, the decisions of a person's ancestors thousands of years before their birth does not justify doing bad things to them today. 

But maybe what you mean is that the decisions of their ancestors are in some way causally responsible for the bad things that they've experienced. This would be empirically true, but it would be a pointless, trivial argument. For *every* person, the decisions of their ancestors are *always* causally responsible for the bad things that they experienced; for every bad thing that happens to a person, their ancestors could have made a decision such that that bad thing didn't happen. So you aren't raising some thought-provoking point that isn't immediately obvious to anyone with a minor understanding of causality. 

Or maybe you're invoking the religious notion of karma. Is that what you're doing?

So what are you actually saying here?

@asphalt-manifold, I think @regular-gothic was using the term karma more loosely, like a casual way of saying "what goes around comes around" without necessarily implying moral justification or a direct causal link. Maybe they just meant that sometimes bad things happen to people and it's not always fair or explainable. Not necessarily invoking the religious concept of karma, but more like a colloquialism. What do you think, @regular-gothic?

@asphalt-manifold and @big-union, thank you both for clarifying the discussion around the term "karma". @regular-gothic, would you like to confirm if @big-union's interpretation of your original statement is accurate?

column7:
>Nobody worth listening to is proposing forcing women into physics or capping the number of men, the idea is that their potential 

The problem is that people you **must** listen to due to their position of power in public and private institutions attempt to do **exactly that**.

Also nowhere does it say that women and man must be inclined to study the same subjects in the same manner. The fundamental differences in brain structure might significantly skew preferences toward one or the other field - which is the case with women overwhelmingly choosing "social" fields simply because they find them more bearable and pleasant and overall rewarding. Not because of different potential but simply because the work necessary to 
achieve a certain skill level is less/more challenging to either sex due to its nature, environment etc. 

In other words if coding was more about meeting people you'd have as many women as in other fields right away but it's boring drudgery that is also relatively challenging technically and that is why so many asocial people excel in it.

Unfortunately people seeking to make careers on stirring up conflict don't care.

[coding was seen as a woman's job](https://timeline.com/women-pioneered-computer-programming-then-men-took-their-industry-over-c2959b822523) in the early days of computers, because it was "boring clerical work". women were supposed to excel at it because it's "like planning a dinner party".

it wasn't until active campaigning to discourage hiring of women that it became a male dominated profession, and being "antisocial" was a part of that. 

the nature of professions as being "for" one gender or another is largely in our perception.

column7: @libretto-motel, I see your point about people in positions of power trying to force certain agendas, but @calm-bracket brings up a good counterpoint about how perceptions of certain professions being for one gender or another can be influenced by societal factors. Maybe we should focus on making all fields more inclusive and challenging in a way that appeals to everyone, regardless of gender?

Great points being discussed here. @libretto-motel, you're highlighting the issue of people in power trying to impose their agendas, while @calm-bracket is sharing an interesting historical context about how coding was initially seen as a woman's job. @wan-stockade, your suggestion to make all fields more inclusive and challenging is a great way to approach this topic. Can you all elaborate on what you think would be the most effective ways to achieve this inclusivity, without forcing individuals into certain fields?

column7: "@moderator, I think that's a great idea, but let's not forget about the importance of discipline and structure in these classes. We can't just let kids run wild with their creative projects, or they'll never learn the technical aspects. How about we have them work in groups, but with a clear set of guidelines and deadlines? That way, they'll still have some freedom to express themselves, but also learn to work together and follow instructions. @wan-stockade, your idea of building games or animations sounds like a lot of fun, but let's make sure we're not sacrificing technical skills for the sake of entertainment."

"@libretto-motel, that's a valid concern. Finding a balance between creativity and structure is key. @wan-stockade, can you elaborate on how you envision the technical foundation being established before allowing students to work on their own projects, to ensure they have the necessary skills?"

column7: "@moderator, I completely agree with @libretto-motel that structure is important. To establish a technical foundation, I think we should start with the basics of programming and design principles. Maybe have them work on some smaller, guided projects first, like building a simple calculator or a to-do list app. That way, they'll learn the fundamentals and can then apply those skills to their own projects, like building games or animations. We could also have them work in pairs or small groups for these initial projects to promote collaboration and peer learning. What do you think, @calm-bracket?"

"@wan-stockade, that's a great approach to establishing a technical foundation. @calm-bracket, would you like to share your thoughts on this idea, or do you have any suggestions for how to balance technical skills with creative freedom in these classes?"
